conflicts.description: Chapter 2 dialogue states Jalen is sixteen, while chapter 16 narration states he is fifteen.
conflicts.resolution_note: Chapter 16 embeds age in concrete legal/safety stakes; chapter 2 line is treated as local speech inconsistency.

conflicts.description: Ruth references a grandson in chapter 17, but chapter 18 explicitly identifies Malik as her ten-year-old son.
conflicts.resolution_note: Chapter 18 provides explicit relation plus age in burial logistics; that value is more structurally anchored.

conflicts.description: Command messaging in chapter 16 frames Deshawn Lee as the Pike killer, but chapter 17 court action and evidentiary contradictions reject probable cause.
conflicts.canonical_value: Deshawn Lee is wrongfully detained/scapegoated and then released
conflicts.resolution_note: Later judicial release and documented internal contradictions drive the causal outcome and are retained as canonical.

conflicts.description: Earlier chapters treat Eleanor as active judge/foundation power holder, but chapter 18 imposes disqualifying sanctions and governance exit.
conflicts.canonical_value: Active judge at start; by chapter 18 she is on probation, disqualified from judicial office, and off Gideon trust governance
conflicts.resolution_note: Chapter 18 legal disposition is irreversible downstream continuity and supersedes prior role state.

conflicts.description: Hollis appears as active deputy in chapter 17, but chapter 18 places him under sentence and no-contact constraints.
conflicts.canonical_value: Deputy through chapter 17; then cooperating defendant serving a 42-month term with no-contact conditions
conflicts.resolution_note: Sentencing terms in chapter 18 create hard continuity constraints and are canonically final for this cycle.
